title: Regulation of cell cycle
description: Test your knowledge of regulation of the cell cycle!

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

The diagram above depicts how the eukaryotic cell cycle is regulated by cyclins and cyclin-dependent kinases (CDKs). 

**Based on the diagram, which of the following best describes the effect of a toxin that prevents the production of the G$_1$ cyclin?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A circular diagram of the cell cycle is labeled. A structure labeled CDK has an arrow labeled G1 and that arrow is joined by an arrow pointing from a triangle labeled G1 cyclin. The 2 arrows point to the CDK attached to the triangle with the label Activated G1 cyclin CDK complex. From this complex an arrow labeled S points to CDK and there is an arrow that branches off of the S arrow and points to several small triangles labeled degraded G1 cyclin. The CDK has an arrow labeled G2 that is joined by an arrow from a circle with the label Mitotic cyclin. The G2 arrow and the mitotic cyclin arrow point to an image of the circle with the CDK attached to it and is labeled activated mitotic cyclin CDK complex. From the activated mitotic cyclin complex an arrow labeled mitosis points back to the CDK that started the circular cycle. Branching off the arrow labeled mitosis is an arrow pointing to several small circles with the label degraded mitotic cyclin.

choices.content: DNA replication would be inhibited, causing the cell to stop dividing.
The cell cycle checkpoints would be ignored, causing the cell to become cancerous.
The mitotic cyclin would functionally replace G$_1$ cyclin, allowing the cell to continue dividing normally.
The transition into mitosis would be blocked, causing the cell to stop dividing.

assessment_items.question_content:
Cyclin B is a mitotic cyclin that helps control the progression of cells into and out of mitosis during the cell cycle. During G$_2$ phase, cyclin B binds to and activates CDK1, a cyclin-dependent kinase. The resulting cyclin B/CDK1 complexes are then transferred into the nucleus, where they trigger entry into mitosis.

In order for cells to exit mitosis, the cyclin B/CDK1 complexes must be inactivated. This occurs through the selective degradation of cyclin B by specific enzymes (CDK1 remains intact). Once the levels of cyclin B are sufficiently reduced, cells transition out of mitosis and into G$_1$ phase, ready to undergo another cell cycle.

**Based on the information above, which of the following best describes the effect of a toxin that prevents the degradation of cyclin B during the cell cycle?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Cells would be arrested in mitosis, and cell division would stop.
Cells would be blocked from entering mitosis, and cell division would stop.
Cells would transition out of mitosis and into G$_0$ phase, and cell division would stop.
Cells would undergo mitosis repeatedly, becoming cancerous.

assessment_items.question_content:
Human papillomavirus (HPV) is a viral infection that is transmitted mainly through skin-to-skin contact. Following transmission, HPV enters host cells and integrates into the host genome, eventually causing the cells to synthesize the viral protein E6. In turn, E6 inactivates the host protein p53, a tumor suppressor that arrests the cell cycle or induces apoptosis when DNA damage is detected.

**Which of the following claims is best supported by the information above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: HPV causes uncontrolled cell division in infected cells.
HPV increases the likelihood of apoptosis in infected cells.
HPV causes infected cells to exit the cell cycle and enter G$_0$ phase.
HPV increases the synthesis of tumor suppressor proteins in infected cells.

assessment_items.question_content:
Adenoviruses are a family of highly contagious viruses that most commonly cause illnesses of the respiratory system, such as bronchitis or pneumonia. In the first phase of an adenovirus infection, the host cell expresses several viral proteins. One of these proteins, E1B-55k, inhibits the activity of the host protein p53, a tumor suppressor that arrests the cell cycle or induces apoptosis when DNA damage is detected.

**Which of the following claims is best supported by the information above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: E1B-55k decreases the likelihood of apoptosis in infected cells.
E1B-55k increases the likelihood of cell cycle arrest in infected cells.
E1B-55k causes infected cells to undergo programmed cell death.
E1B-55k causes infected cells to transition out of the cell cycle and into G$_0$ phase.

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

The diagram above represents a model of a signal transduction pathway in a cell that is targeted by two different ligands, L$_1$ and L$_2$. When L$_1$ binds with membrane receptor R$_1$, the pathway is activated, and CDK activity in the cell increases. Alternatively, when L$_2$ binds with membrane receptor R$_2$, downstream signaling by the pathway is inhibited.

**Based on the model above, which of the following mutations is most likely to result in cancerous cell growth?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: At the top of the diagram Ligand 1, L1 is shown as a small box and Ligand 2 is shown as a small diamond. Receptor 1 is shown as a larger box with a the corner of the box cut out and Receptor 2 is shown as a box with the top shaped like one half of a diamond. There is an arrow from Ligand 1 pointing to Receptor 1 and the small box fits into the corner of Receptor 1. There is an arrow pointing from Ligand 2, and the diamond fits into the top of Receptor 2. There is an arrow from Receptor 1 that points towards the caption signal transduction pathway, and from there the final arrow points to the caption Increased CDK activity.  There is aline from Ligand 2 going towards signal transduction pathway, indicating Ligand 2 blocks the signal transduction pathway

choices.content: A mutation that prevents the binding of L$_2$ with R$_2$
A mutation that prevents the binding of L$_1$ with R$_1$
A mutation that permanently binds L$_2$ with R$_2$
A mutation that allows L$_1$ to functionally replace L$_2$

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

The diagram above represents a model of a signal transduction pathway in a cell that is targeted by two different ligands, L$_1$ and L$_2$. When L$_1$ binds with membrane receptor R$_1$, the pathway is activated, and CDK activity in the cell increases. Alternatively, when L$_2$ binds with membrane receptor R$_2$, downstream signaling by the pathway is inhibited.

**Based on the model above, what would most likely result if a mutation prevents the binding of L$_2$ with R$_2$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: At the top of the diagram Ligand 1, L1 is shown as a small box and Ligand 2 is shown as a small diamond. Receptor 1 is shown as a larger box with a the corner of the box cut out and Receptor 2 is shown as a box with the top shaped like one half of a diamond. There is an arrow from Ligand 1 pointing to Receptor 1 and the small box fits into the corner of Receptor 1. There is an arrow pointing from Ligand 2, and the diamond fits into the top of Receptor 2. There is an arrow from Receptor 1 that points towards the caption signal transduction pathway, and from there the final arrow points to the caption Increased CDK activity.  There is aline from Ligand 2 going towards signal transduction pathway, indicating Ligand 2 blocks the signal transduction pathway

choices.content: The signal transduction pathway would proceed unchecked, resulting in increased cell division.
The signal transduction pathway would proceed unchecked, resulting in decreased cell division.
The signal transduction pathway would be inhibited, resulting in increased cell division.
The signal transduction pathway would be inhibited, resulting in decreased cell division.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following mutated proteins is most likely to cause a cell to become cancerous?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: An overactive version of a protein that stimulates cell division
An overactive version of a protein that promotes apoptosis
An underactive version of a protein that stimulates growth hormone receptors
An underactive version of a protein that promotes cell cycle progression

assessment_items.question_content:
A genetic mutation causes a somatic cell to become cancerous. 

**Which of the following is the most likely explanation for how the mutation caused this malignant transformation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The mutation created an underactive version of a protein that promotes apoptosis.
The mutation created an overactive version of a protein that inhibits cell division.
The mutation created an underactive version of a protein that promotes cell division.
The mutation created an overactive version of a protein that prevents cell cycle progression.
